# Marketing Plan: Rubix Edge Controller — AU Market

**Target Audience:** Systems Integrators (Building Automation / IoT)
**Budget:** $10k–$50k | **Timeline:** Q3 2026 (1 Quarter) | **Primary Channels:** LinkedIn, Email

---

## 1. Key Messaging & Value Propositions

**Core Positioning:**
> Rubix Edge Controller — the open, protocol-agnostic edge platform that lets systems integrators deliver smarter buildings faster, with less vendor lock-in.

**Value Propositions for AU Systems Integrators:**

| Message | Why It Resonates |
|---|---|
| **Protocol-agnostic integration** — BACnet, Modbus, LoRa, REST APIs from a single device | SIs juggle multiple protocols across retrofit and greenfield jobs; one controller simplifies the stack |
| **Reduce project delivery time** — pre-built drivers and a visual configuration layer cut commissioning hours | Time-on-site is the #1 cost driver for AU integrators; faster commissioning = better margins |
| **Open platform, no lock-in** — Linux-based, API-first architecture lets you own the customer relationship | AU SIs are wary of being locked into a single OEM's ecosystem; openness is a competitive differentiator |
| **Local-first edge compute** — data processing and control logic runs on-prem, even when the cloud is unreachable | Critical for AU sites with unreliable connectivity (regional, industrial, remote campuses) |
| **Australian-supported** — NubeIO is AU-based with local engineering, support, and stock | De-risks procurement and support vs. importing from overseas vendors with timezone mismatches |

**Proof Points to Develop:**
- 2–3 AU case studies (e.g. commercial HVAC retrofit, multi-site energy monitoring)
- Quantified stat: "X% reduction in commissioning time" from a real deployment
- Partner testimonial from an existing AU SI

---

## 2. Channel Strategy

### LinkedIn (Primary — ~40% of budget)

| Tactic | Detail | Est. Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Company page content** | 2 posts/week: technical tips, case study snippets, product updates, industry commentary (NABERS, Section J, HVAC trends) | Internal effort |
| **Sponsored content ads** | Target: AU-based job titles — "Systems Integrator", "BMS Engineer", "Controls Engineer", "Building Automation", "HVAC Controls" at companies with 10–200 employees | $4k–$8k/quarter |
| **LinkedIn Lead Gen Forms** | Gate a technical whitepaper or ROI calculator behind a lead form; lower friction than landing pages | Included in ad spend |
| **Founder/team thought leadership** | Personal posts from NubeIO leadership on open-source edge, AU building tech trends | Internal effort |

**Content Calendar Themes (rotate weekly):**
- Week 1: Technical how-to / integration tip
- Week 2: Customer story or use case
- Week 3: Industry trend / regulation commentary
- Week 4: Product feature spotlight

### Email (Primary — ~20% of budget)

| Tactic | Detail | Est. Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Lead nurture sequence** | 5-email drip for new leads: (1) Welcome + positioning, (2) Technical overview PDF, (3) Case study, (4) ROI comparison, (5) Book a demo CTA | $1k–$2k (tooling + copywriting) |
| **Monthly newsletter** | Curated for SIs: product updates, integration guides, AU industry news | Internal effort |
| **Re-engagement campaign** | Target cold leads / past enquiries with a "what's new" angle | Internal effort |
| **Tooling** | Email platform (Mailchimp/Brevo free-tier may suffice at early volumes) | $0–$500/quarter |

### Content Assets (Supporting — ~30% of budget)

| Asset | Purpose | Est. Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Technical whitepaper** — "The SI's Guide to Open Edge Controllers" | Lead magnet for LinkedIn + email | $2k–$4k (freelance writer + internal review) |
| **ROI calculator** (spreadsheet or simple web tool) | Lets SIs estimate commissioning time savings | $1k–$3k |
| **2 × customer case studies** (1-pager each) | Social proof for mid-funnel leads | $1k–$2k |
| **Product comparison sheet** — Rubix vs. Tridium/Honeywell/Schneider | Arms SIs with switching justification | Internal effort |
| **Short demo video** (2–3 min, screen capture + voiceover) | LinkedIn + email embed, landing page | $1k–$2k |

### Events (Supplementary — ~10% of budget)

| Tactic | Detail | Est. Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **1 × webinar** — "Open Edge for SIs: Live Demo + Q&A" | Lead gen + pipeline acceleration; promote via LinkedIn + email | $500–$1k (platform + promotion) |
| **ARBS 2026 / BuildingIQ meetup attendance** | If timing aligns — networking, not a booth. Meet SIs face-to-face | $1k–$2k (travel + collateral) |

---

## 3. Timeline & Milestones

### Month 1 — Foundation

- [ ] Finalise messaging framework and SI persona
- [ ] Set up LinkedIn ad account, audiences, tracking (UTMs, LinkedIn Insight Tag)
- [ ] Set up email platform + lead nurture sequence (5 emails)
- [ ] Commission whitepaper and begin case study interviews
- [ ] Launch LinkedIn organic posting cadence (2x/week)
- [ ] **Milestone:** First LinkedIn ad campaign live by end of Month 1

### Month 2 — Acceleration

- [ ] Publish whitepaper; launch as gated lead magnet on LinkedIn Lead Gen Forms
- [ ] Publish first case study; distribute via email + LinkedIn
- [ ] Launch monthly newsletter (first edition)
- [ ] Record and publish demo video
- [ ] Host webinar — promote 2 weeks in advance
- [ ] **Milestone:** 50+ marketing-qualified leads (MQLs) captured

### Month 3 — Optimisation & Pipeline

- [ ] Publish second case study + comparison sheet
- [ ] Optimise LinkedIn ads based on Month 1–2 data (pause underperformers, scale winners)
- [ ] Run email re-engagement campaign to cold leads
- [ ] Deliver ROI calculator asset
- [ ] Attend 1 industry event / meetup if available
- [ ] **Milestone:** 20+ demo requests or sales conversations from marketing-sourced leads

---

## 4. Success Metrics & KPIs

| Metric | Target (end of quarter) | How to Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Marketing Qualified Leads (MQLs)** | 100–150 | CRM / email platform |
| **Demo requests / sales conversations** | 20–30 | CRM pipeline stage |
| **LinkedIn ad CTR** | >0.8% | LinkedIn Campaign Manager |
| **Cost per lead (CPL)** | <$50 | Ad spend ÷ leads |
| **Email open rate** | >30% | Email platform |
| **Email click-through rate** | >4% | Email platform |
| **Whitepaper downloads** | 50+ | Landing page / Lead Gen Forms |
| **Webinar registrations** | 40+ (25+ attendees) | Webinar platform |
| **Pipeline value generated** | $100k+ (attributed to marketing) | CRM |

---

## Budget Summary

| Category | Low Estimate | High Estimate |
|---|---|---|
| LinkedIn ads | $4,000 | $8,000 |
| Content creation (whitepaper, case studies, video) | $5,000 | $11,000 |
| Email tooling + copywriting | $1,000 | $2,500 |
| Webinar + events | $1,500 | $3,000 |
| **Total** | **$11,500** | **$24,500** |

This leaves headroom within the $50k cap for scaling LinkedIn ads if early results are strong, or for a second webinar / additional content assets in Month 3.

---

**Next steps:** If you'd like, I can drill deeper into any section — LinkedIn ad copy drafts, the email nurture sequence, or the whitepaper outline.

## 1. Key Messaging & Value Propositions

| Enterprise-grade BAS | Enterprise capability, edge-native simplicity |

| Complex commissioning | Deploy in minutes, not days |

**Core Message:**
> "The open, intelligent controller for modern buildings — deploy faster, integrate anything, pay less."

**Three Value Pillars:**
1. **Open by design** — No vendor lock-in. Works with BACnet, Modbus, REST, MQTT, LoRa out of the box.
2. **Edge intelligence** — Run logic locally, manage remotely. Built-in support for AI-driven optimization.
3. **Lower TCO** — Fraction of the cost of legacy controllers with faster commissioning and fewer truck rolls.

- Commissioning time benchmark (minutes vs. hours)

**Segmented lists:**

### Week 3: Educate

- **Milestone:** 10 demo/pilot requests, 300 landing page visits cumulative

### Week 4: Convert

## Key Takeaway from Delta Controls' Approach

Delta Controls wins on **trust, reliability, and ecosystem depth** — built over decades. NubeIO can't out-legacy them. Instead, win on:

The user wants to find: all

## How to Query

Call `rubix.query_filter` with orgId, deviceId, and a `filter` string using Haystack syntax.

## Haystack Filter Syntax

**By type:**
- `type is "core.trigger"` — exact type match
- `type in ["core.trigger", "core.counter"]` — multiple types

**By name:**
- `name contains "Supply"` — substring match
- `name like "valve%"` — wildcard

**By identity tags (fast):**
- `i has "sensor"` — has tag
- `i has ["temp", "humid"]` — has any of these tags
- `i hasAll ["temp", "sensor", "point"]` — has all tags

**By refs:**
- `siteRef is "node_001"` — at a specific site
- `equipRef is "ahu_01"` — belongs to equipment
- `parentRef is "dev_123"` — direct children

**By port values:**
- `out.valueNum > 20` — output value above 20
- `state.value is "active"` — string port value

**By alarm state:**
- `i has "alarm" and state.value is "active"` — active alarms
- `severity.value is "Critical"` — critical alarms

**By settings (JSONB):**
- `settings.productCode contains "widget"` — settings field search

**Combining:**
- `type is "sensor" and name contains "Temp"` — AND
- `i has "temp" or i has "humid"` — OR
- `not (status is "inactive")` — NOT
- `(i has "temp" or i has "humid") and siteRef is "site_1"` — grouping

**Modifiers (pipe):**
- `type is "device" | limit 50` — limit results
- `type is "device" | sort name asc` — sort
- `type is "device" | offset 10 | limit 10` — pagination
- `type is "trigger" | select out, in1` — port projection

## Instructions

1. Translate the user's natural language request into a Haystack filter
2. Call `rubix.query_filter` with the filter
3. Present the results in a clear table or list
4. If no results, suggest alternative filters

Let me get started with Rubix! First, let me check the API info and available auth tools.# Getting Started with Rubix

Rubix is a **Building Automation** platform that lets you query nodes, build flows, analyze history, and create dashboards.

---

## Step 1: Authentication

You need to log in first. Rubix supports two login methods:

| Method | When to use |
|--------|------------|
| **PIN Login** (recommended) | Log in with your Org ID + 6-digit PIN |
| **Email/Password** | Log in with email and password |

**To log in, provide one of the following:**

- **PIN Login**: Your `orgId` and 6-digit `pin`
- **Email Login**: Your `email` and `password`

---

## Step 2: Discover the System

Once authenticated, you can explore using these available prompts:

| Prompt | Description |
|--------|------------|
| `/rubix.navigation` | Build site navigation — sites, buildings, floors |
| `/rubix.query_nodes` | Query nodes using the Rubix Haystack filter language |
| `/rubix.create_flow` | Create nodes and connect them with edges for automation |
| `/rubix.history_analysis` | Query and analyze historical time-series data |
| `/rubix.build_dashboard` | Create dashboard pages with widgets and templates |
| `/rubix.tagging` | Manage tags and identity markers on nodes |

---

## Step 3: Understand the Node Hierarchy

Rubix organizes everything as **nodes** in a hierarchy:

```
Org → Site → Building → Floor → Equipment → Points
```

You can query, tag, and automate across this structure.

---

## Ready to begin?

Please provide your login credentials to get started:
- **PIN login**: Give me your Org ID and 6-digit PIN
- **Email login**: Give me your email and password

Once logged in, I can help you explore your system, query nodes, build flows, or create dashboards.

## IMPORTANT: Auth is automatic. NEVER ask for orgId or deviceId. The login gives you both.

## Step 1: Show what exists

First call `rubix.runtime_status` (no params needed) to get the system context.

Then call `rubix.query_nodes` with filter `type is "ui.container"` to see all existing navigation containers.

Present the results as a tree. Look at parentId to determine hierarchy:
```
Site: Sydney Office (id: xxx)
  Building: Tower A (id: yyy)
    Floor: Level 1 (id: zzz)
    Floor: Level 2 (id: aaa)
```

## If user wants to CREATE new navigation

Use `rubix.create_node` for each level. It handles auth and parentRef automatically.

**Site (top level, no parentName needed):**
```json
{"type": "ui.container", "name": "Sydney Office", "tags": "site", "settings": "{\"containerType\": \"site\"}"}
```

**Building (under a site — use parentName):**
```json
{"type": "ui.container", "name": "Tower A", "parentName": "Sydney Office", "tags": "building"}
```

**Floor (under a building):**
```json
{"type": "ui.container", "name": "Level 1", "parentName": "Tower A", "tags": "floor"}
```

Create top-down: site first, then buildings, then floors. Each `create_node` returns the new node's ID.

## If user wants to EDIT existing

Use `rubix.get_node` with the node name to find it, then `rubix.update_node` to change it:
- Rename: `{"name": "Floor 1", "newName": "Ground Floor"}`
- Move: `{"name": "Level 1", "settings": "{\"containerType\": \"zone\"}"}`

Use `rubix.delete_node` to remove (cascades to children).

## If user just wants to SEE navigation

Call `rubix.query_nodes` with these filters:
- All sites: `i has "site"`
- All buildings: `i has "building"`
- All floors: `i has "floor"`
- Everything: `type is "ui.container"`

Present as a clear hierarchy tree.
